From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| Born | August 5, 1972 (age 53) [Taylorsville, North Carolina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taylorsville,_North_Carolina "Taylorsville, North Carolina"), U.S. |

**Onur Tukel** (born August 5, 1972) is a [Turkish-American](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turkish_American "Turkish American")[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Onur_Tukel#cite_note-1) actor, painter, and filmmaker. A notable figure in the [New York City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")[independent film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") community, Tukel's films often deal with issues of gender and relationships.

In 1997, Tukel wrote and directed his first feature film _House of Pancakes_. His subsequent film, the vampire drama _Drawing Blood_, was completed in 1999. In a 2014 interview with _[Entertainment Weekly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entertainment\_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")_, Tukel recalled that _Drawing Blood_ was "the only time [he] made money on a movie."[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Onur_Tukel#cite_note-2) His next film, the comedy drama _Ding-a-ling-Less_, was completed in 2001. In 2005, Tukel (credited as Sergio Lapel) also wrote and directed the comedy _The Pigs_ about a group of middle age men who arrange to have their wives murdered.

In 2012, Tukel wrote, produced, directed, and starred in the comedy drama film _[Richard's Wedding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard%27s\_Wedding "Richard's Wedding")_, which featured such other independent filmmakers as [Josephine Decker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Josephine_Decker "Josephine Decker"), [Lawrence Michael Levine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lawrence_Michael_Levine "Lawrence Michael Levine"), and [Jennifer Prediger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jennifer_Prediger "Jennifer Prediger").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Onur_Tukel#cite_note-3) Despite an overall mixed critical response, the film was praised by David DeWitt of _[The New York Times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_New\_York\_Times "The New York Times")_ as "a slice of the [John Cassavetes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Cassavetes "John Cassavetes"), [John Sayles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Sayles "John Sayles") and [Richard Linklater](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Linklater "Richard Linklater") life."[[4]](htt

...[truncated at 3000 chars]"}]
